title: How far a name reaches
checkLabels.t8: Below four there is no discount
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: The console shows nothing. The first line crashes and stops everything after it.
why.not-linked: The script never reaches the page.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel below. It says which name does not exist.
hint3.crashed: discount is not defined means the name only exists inside the curly brackets of the if. Outside them it is gone.
hint4.crashed: Declare it before the if, with the value 0. Inside the if only change its value.
why.wrong-order: order does not give the right numbers.
hint2.wrong-order: Four large at 5 is 20, minus ten percent leaves 18. You are getting something smaller.
hint3.wrong-order: Look at which name you pass to cupPrice. There is a similar name at the top of the file.
hint4.wrong-order: The function receives the size through its own brackets. That is the one that has to go into cupPrice, not the one outside.
why.wrong-discount: The discount is given when it should not be.
hint2.wrong-discount: Three cups is below the threshold. Three large cost 15, with nothing added and nothing taken off.
hint3.wrong-discount: The starting value before the if has to be zero. The if raises it only at four and above.
hint2.empty-loyalty: At zero the loop does not run once. Its body never executes.
hint3.empty-loyalty: If the name is declared inside the loop, at zero rounds it is never created. You get undefined.
hint4.empty-loyalty: Declare the counter before the loop, with the value 0. Inside, only change it.
hint2.var-leak: var does not respect curly brackets. That is exactly why the code looked almost fine.
hint3.var-leak: let and const only reach the closing bracket of their own block. That is stricter, and therefore safer.
why.wrong-count: There are not three lines in the console.
why.lost-p: A paragraph has disappeared from the page.
blocks.0.text: A total with a discount and a stamp counter. The code is written. The console is empty.
blocks.1.text: It is not empty because there is nothing to show. It is empty because the first line crashed and took the other two down with it.
blocks.3.text: Declare a name with let or const inside curly brackets and it lives only until the closing bracket. Outside it does not exist. Not empty, not undefined — simply not there.
blocks.4.text: That is why declaring inside an if is a trap. You find out only when you need it further down.
blocks.5.quote: Decide what has to survive the bracket. That is what gets declared before it.
blocks.6.text: var does not respect the block
blocks.7.text: The second function does not crash. It returns a number and looks fine. The reason is var — it leaks out of the curly brackets and hangs around inside the function.
blocks.9.text: stock returns the last n and works. It works until days is zero. Then the loop never runs, last is never created, and the function returns undefined. Nothing crashes.
blocks.10.text: A name that is not yours
blocks.11.text: A function can see names from outside too. So if you get the parameter wrong, the code does not crash — something else with the same name is found and used instead.
blocks.12.text: The result comes out, looks plausible, and is wrong. This is the most expensive of the three mistakes here, because it leaves no trace.
blocks.14.text: Fix all three places. order has to apply the discount only at four cups and above, and use the size it was given.
blocks.15.text: loyalty has to return 0 for zero visits. Not a single var should be left in the whole file.
